{{wikidata link|Q17221560}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記| date = 2024年12月}} {{神社 |名称= 矢代寸神社 |画像= [[File:Yashirogi Shrine.jpg|270px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地= {{jalink|大阪府|Q122723}}{{jalink|岸和田市|Q740456}}八田町357番地 |位置= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |祭神= 武内宿祢、波多八代宿祢
(合祀) 建御名方命、素盞嗚命、菅原道真、市杵島比売命、武甕槌命 |社格= {{jalink|式内社}}、旧{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}} |創建= {{jalink|継体天皇|Q349277}}元年(西暦503年) |本殿= {{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |社殿= 本殿、幣殿、拝殿、神撰所、神輿庫、神庫 |別名= 一ノ宮 |例祭= 10月10日 |神事= だんじり、御神輿 |地図= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=矢代寸神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''矢代寸神社'''(やしろぎじんじゃ)は、{{jalink|大阪府|Q122723}}{{jalink|岸和田市|Q740456}}{{jalink|八田町 (岸和田市)||八田町}}357番地にある神社。{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}には{{jalink|従五位|Q11071125}}屋代村社と載せ又当地は屋代村（矢代寸）と称し、矢代寸神社ニ座あり、一座は本社にして一ノ宮と称し現在地の大字八田字宮内にあり、一座は諏訪宮また矢代寸下神社と称し同市の{{jalink|神須屋町}}にあった。江戸時代は{{jalink|岸和田藩|Q3034469}}の一ノ宮にて藩主交代の際は藩下各社社に拝礼するのを例としていたが、後に当社のみに拝礼するようになった。歴史のある神社だか、現在は常時神主はいなく、近くの{{jalink|土生神社}}の宮司（阪井氏）が兼任している。 === 歴史 === *{{jalink|503年|Q30975}}({{jalink|継体天皇|Q349277}}元年)　鎮座する。 *{{jalink|1090年|Q19466}}({{jalink|寛治|Q1193874}}4年)　正月に{{jalink|白河天皇|Q60949|白河上皇}}が{{jalink|熊野御幸}}の折参拝される。 *{{jalink|1640年|Q6901}}({{jalink|寛永|Q1192913}}17年)　{{jalink|岡部宣勝|Q11473826}}が{{jalink|摂津国|Q864163}}{{jalink|高槻藩|Q10863415}}より入封し、当社を{{jalink|和泉国|Q1047117}}{{jalink|岸和田藩|Q3034469}}の一ノ宮とした。　 *{{jalink|1737年|Q6993}}({{jalink|元文|Q1145754}}2年)　{{jalink|吉田家|Q11413722|京都吉田家}}に願い出により「{{jalink|牛頭天王|Q11570247|一宮牛頭天王}}(いちのみやごずてんのう)」を称される。 *{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}({{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}6年)　{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}に指定される。 *{{jalink|1907年|Q2048}}({{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}40年)　{{jalink|泉南郡|Q1205635}}{{jalink|有真香村|Q11516636}}神須屋の諏訪神社を合祀する。{{jalink|内務省 (日本)|Q1142265|内務省}}神社・宗教両局長依命通牒によるいわゆる「{{jalink|神社合祀|Q11590700}}」で泉南郡有真香村の神須屋・真上、同郡{{jalink|土生郷村|Q11423777}}極楽寺・流木・畑の神を合祀した。土生郷村土生にある{{jalink|土生神社}}にも合祀せよとの方針が示されたが、{{jalink|山原作太郎}}氏の反対運動によってこの合祀方針を断念させた。また畑の波多神社も合祀されるものの、後に復社される。 *{{jalink|1908年|Q2056}}(明治41年)　{{jalink|神饌幣帛料供進社}}に指定される。 === 一ノ宮の由来 === 古来より阿間河荘{{jalink|惣社}}として一ノ宮と呼ばれていた。江戸時代になり、岡部宣勝が高槻藩より岸和田藩に入封した際、領内には一ノ宮がなかった。領内の南郡{{jalink|岸和田浜町|Q11475839}}には、和泉国四ノ宮と格式の高い{{jalink|積川神社|Q11597137}}の{{jalink|御旅所|Q11489117}}があったが、積川神社鎮座地の同郡積川村は幕府領であった。このため、同郡小松里村字北額（{{jalink|山城国|Q749276}}{{jalink|淀藩|Q844014}}領）にあり、白河上皇の{{jalink|扁額|Q5358873}}で知られる、積川神社の{{jalink|遥拝所}}に御旅所を移転させ、その際、領内で古く格式の高い神社を探したところ、矢代寸神社が該当し、岸和田藩の一ノ宮とした。{{jalink|藩主交代}}や{{jalink|参勤交代|Q1151844}}の際には、藩主自ら岡部家の{{jalink|菩提寺|Q1084697}}である{{jalink|泉光寺|Q17222155}}とともに参拝された。{{jalink|廃藩置県|Q331905}}により岸和田藩が消滅に伴い、藩の一ノ宮ではなくなったが、地元民により{{jalink|阿間河谷}}の一ノ宮として今でも祀られている。 === 祭神 === *{{jalink|武内宿禰|Q1073668}}(たけうちのすくね) *{{jalink|波多氏 (古代)|Q11555455|波多八代宿禰}}(はたやしろのすくね) *{{jalink|建御名方命}}(たけみなかたのかみ)　‐　神須屋社({{jalink|諏訪神社|Q2370082}})より合祀 *{{jalink|素盞嗚命}}(すさのおのみこと)　‐　真上社({{jalink|弥栄神社|Q11487447}})より合祀 *{{jalink|菅原道真|Q382005}}(すがわらのみちざね) ‐　神須屋社({{jalink|天神神社|Q11443287}})より合祀 *{{jalink|市杵島比売命}}(いちきしまひめ)　‐ 極楽寺社({{jalink|厳島神社|Q191763}})より合祀 *{{jalink|武甕槌命}}(たけみかづち)　‐　流木社({{jalink|鹿島神社|Q11677154}})より合祀 *祭神不詳だか流木社より四十九神社({{jalink|落合神社}})が合祀されている。 - 戦でなくなった四十九人の首を埋めたという伝説がある。 === 例祭 === 10月の{{jalink|体育の日}}に{{jalink|岸和田十月祭礼|Q11475809}}で東岸和田だんじり祭りの{{jalink|宮入}}が行われる。 地車は{{jalink|八田町 (岸和田市)||八田町}}・{{jalink|真上町}}・{{jalink|神須屋町}}・{{jalink|極楽寺町}}・{{jalink|流木町}}計5台の宮入する。 だんじり祭りにはとても珍しい{{jalink|神輿渡御}}がある。 だんじりの宮出しに行われ、神輿担ぐのは当番町(最後に宮入をする町)であり五町持ち回りで、だんじりはお神輿に続いて{{jalink|御旅所|Q11489117}}までお供する。 御旅所は諏訪神社跡(神須屋町)にある。 先代のお神輿は{{jalink|岸城神社|Q119929652}}が造ったが神輿を担ぐには格式が低かったため、矢代寸神社が購入したもの。 === 境内社 === *{{jalink|稲荷神社|Q514480}} - {{jalink|白高大明神}}と{{jalink|光良大明神}}が祀られている。 *{{jalink|道祖神|Q269515|道祖神社}}(足神様) ‐ 足の悪い人、痛い人が石を撫でてたら治るとされている。 *{{jalink|弁財天}} - 流木社({{jalink|弁財天社}})より{{jalink|遷宮|Q2271023}} === アクセス === {{jalink|南海本線|Q59453}}{{jalink|岸和田駅|Q6416611}}又は{{jalink|阪和線|Q1154800}}{{jalink|東岸和田駅|Q857440}}より{{jalink|南海ウイングバス|Q11408142}}641,642,643,644系統に乗車し、バス停「一の宮」下車 バス停より南へ徒歩3分 === 脚注 === {{Reflist}} === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|岸和田十月祭礼|Q11475809}} *{{jalink|阿理莫神社}} - 大阪府貝塚市久保に鎮座。阿間河荘の豪族である安幕(アマカ)氏由来の神社。鎮座が507年で当社ととても近い。 *{{jalink|行遇堂|Q17225570}}（神須屋町) ‐ 旧{{jalink|阿間河荘}}の神事が行われる時下字（極楽寺・畑・流木）と上字（八田・神須屋・真上）がこのお堂で行き合って矢代寸神社にお参りしていた。 *{{jalink|真上八大龍王神社}}（真上町) ‐ 旧弥栄神社の跡地に土取りをしていた人が埋もれてなくなる事故が起きたことから、戦後{{jalink|八大龍王}}と{{jalink|産土神|Q1392097}}をお祀りされている。 *{{jalink|海岸寺跡}}(貝塚市半田町) ‐ 岸和田藩岡部宣勝が徳川家を供養するために建立した寺院。明治2年に廃寺。一圓法師大徳の墓石に阿間河一宮(矢代寸神社)・第十一世と刻まれている。 {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:やしろきしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=大阪府}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Autogenerated pages with jawiki interwikis, possibly accidentally overwritten]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

極楽寺社({{jalink|厳島神社|Q191763}})より合祀 *{{jalink|武甕槌命}}(たけみかづち)　‐　流木社({{jalink|鹿島神社|Q11677154}})より合祀 *祭神不詳だか流木社より四十九神社({{jalink|落合神社}})が合祀されている。 - 戦でなくなった四十九人の首を埋めたという伝説がある。 === 例祭 === 10月の{{jalink|体育の日}}に{{jalink|岸和田十月祭礼|Q11475809}}で東岸和田だんじり祭りの{{jalink|宮入}}が行われる。 地車は{{jalink|八田町 (岸和田市)||八田町}}・{{jalink|真上町}}・{{jalink|神須屋町}}・{{jalink|極楽寺町}}・{{jalink|流木町}}計5台の宮入する。 だんじり祭りにはとても珍しい{{jalink|神輿渡御}}がある。 だんじりの宮出しに行われ、神輿担ぐのは当番町(最後に宮入をする町)であり五町持ち回りで、だんじりはお神輿に続いて{{jalink|御旅所|Q11489117}}までお供する。 御旅所は諏訪神社跡(神須屋町)にある。 先代のお神輿は{{jalink|岸城神社|Q119929652}}が造ったが神輿を担ぐには格式が低かったため、矢代寸神社が購入したもの。 === 境内社 === *{{jalink|稲荷神社|Q514480}} - {{jalink|白高大明神}}と{{jalink|光良大明神}}が祀られている。 *{{jalink|道祖神|Q269515|道祖神社}}(足神様) ‐ 足の悪い人、痛い人が石を撫でてたら治るとされている。 *{{jalink|弁財天}} - 流木社({{jalink|弁財天社}})より{{jalink|遷宮|Q2271023}} === アクセス === {{jalink|南海本線|Q59453}}{{jalink|岸和田駅|Q6416611}}又は{{jalink|阪和線|Q1154800}}{{jalink|東岸和田駅|Q857440}}より{{jalink|南海ウイングバス|Q11408142}}641,642,643,644系統に乗車し、バス停「一の宮」下車 バス停より南へ徒歩3分 === 脚注 === {{Reflist}} === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|岸和田十月祭礼|Q11475809}} *{{jalink|阿理莫神社}} - 大阪府貝塚市久保に鎮座。阿間河荘の豪族である安幕(アマカ)氏由来の神社。鎮座が507年で当社ととても近い。 *{{jalink|行遇堂|Q17225570}}（神須屋町) ‐ 旧{{jalink|阿間河荘}}の神事が行われる時下字（極楽寺・畑・流木）と上字（八田・神須屋・真上）がこのお堂で行き合って矢代寸神社にお参りしていた。 *{{jalink|真上八大龍王神社}}（真上町) ‐ 旧弥栄神社の跡地に土取りをしていた人が埋もれてなくなる事故が起きたことから、戦後{{jalink|八大龍王}}と{{jalink|産土神|Q1392097}}をお祀りされている。 *{{jalink|海岸寺跡}}(貝塚市半田町) ‐ 岸和田藩岡部宣勝が徳川家を供養するために建立した寺院。明治2年に廃寺。一圓法師大徳の墓石に阿間河一宮(矢代寸神社)・第十一世と刻まれている。 {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:やしろきしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=大阪府}} == Categories == Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]] [[Category:need translation]]

{{wikidata link|Q17221560}} == Japanese content == {{出典の明記| date = 2024年12月}} {{神社 |名称= 矢代寸神社 |画像= [[File:Yashirogi Shrine.jpg|270px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地= {{jalink|大阪府|Q122723}}{{jalink|岸和田市|Q740456}}八田町357番地 |位置= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |祭神= 武内宿祢、波多八代宿祢
(合祀) 建御名方命、素盞嗚命、菅原道真、市杵島比売命、武甕槌命 |社格= {{jalink|式内社}}、旧{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}} |創建= {{jalink|継体天皇|Q349277}}元年(西暦503年) |本殿= {{jalink|流造|Q210684}} |社殿= 本殿、幣殿、拝殿、神撰所、神輿庫、神庫 |別名= 一ノ宮 |例祭= 10月10日 |神事= だんじり、御神輿 |地図= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=矢代寸神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''矢代寸神社'''(やしろぎじんじゃ)は、{{jalink|大阪府|Q122723}}{{jalink|岸和田市|Q740456}}{{jalink|八田町 (岸和田市)||八田町}}357番地にある神社。{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}には{{jalink|従五位|Q11071125}}屋代村社と載せ又当地は屋代村（矢代寸）と称し、矢代寸神社ニ座あり、一座は本社にして一ノ宮と称し現在地の大字八田字宮内にあり、一座は諏訪宮また矢代寸下神社と称し同市の{{jalink|神須屋町}}にあった。江戸時代は{{jalink|岸和田藩|Q3034469}}の一ノ宮にて藩主交代の際は藩下各社社に拝礼するのを例としていたが、後に当社のみに拝礼するようになった。歴史のある神社だか、現在は常時神主はいなく、近くの{{jalink|土生神社}}の宮司（阪井氏）が兼任している。 === 歴史 === *{{jalink|503年|Q30975}}({{jalink|継体天皇|Q349277}}元年)　鎮座する。 *{{jalink|1090年|Q19466}}({{jalink|寛治|Q1193874}}4年)　正月に{{jalink|白河天皇|Q60949|白河上皇}}が{{jalink|熊野御幸}}の折参拝される。 *{{jalink|1640年|Q6901}}({{jalink|寛永|Q1192913}}17年)　{{jalink|岡部宣勝|Q11473826}}が{{jalink|摂津国|Q864163}}{{jalink|高槻藩|Q10863415}}より入封し、当社を{{jalink|和泉国|Q1047117}}{{jalink|岸和田藩|Q3034469}}の一ノ宮とした。　 *{{jalink|1737年|Q6993}}({{jalink|元文|Q1145754}}2年)　{{jalink|吉田家|Q11413722|京都吉田家}}に願い出により「{{jalink|牛頭天王|Q11570247|一宮牛頭天王}}(いちのみやごずてんのう)」を称される。 *{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}({{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}6年)　{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}に指定される。 *{{jalink|1907年|Q2048}}({{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}40年)　{{jalink|泉南郡|Q1205635}}{{jalink|有真香村|Q11516636}}神須屋の諏訪神社を合祀する。{{jalink|内務省 (日本)|Q1142265|内務省}}神社・宗教両局長依命通牒によるいわゆる「{{jalink|神社合祀|Q11590700}}」で泉南郡有真香村の神須屋・真上、同郡{{jalink|土生郷村|Q11423777}}極楽寺・流木・畑の神を合祀した。土生郷村土生にある{{jalink|土生神社}}にも合祀せよとの方針が示されたが、{{jalink|山原作太郎}}氏の反対運動によってこの合祀方針を断念させた。また畑の波多神社も合祀されるものの、後に復社される。 *{{jalink|1908年|Q2056}}(明治41年)　{{jalink|神饌幣帛料供進社}}に指定される。 === 一ノ宮の由来 === 古来より阿間河荘{{jalink|惣社}}として一ノ宮と呼ばれていた。江戸時代になり、岡部宣勝が高槻藩より岸和田藩に入封した際、領内には一ノ宮がなかった。領内の南郡{{jalink|岸和田浜町|Q11475839}}には、和泉国四ノ宮と格式の高い{{jalink|積川神社|Q11597137}}の{{jalink|御旅所|Q11489117}}があったが、積川神社鎮座地の同郡積川村は幕府領であった。このため、同郡小松里村字北額（{{jalink|山城国|Q749276}}{{jalink|淀藩|Q844014}}領）にあり、白河上皇の{{jalink|扁額|Q5358873}}で知られる、積川神社の{{jalink|遥拝所}}に御旅所を移転させ、その際、領内で古く格式の高い神社を探したところ、矢代寸神社が該当し、岸和田藩の一ノ宮とした。{{jalink|藩主交代}}や{{jalink|参勤交代|Q1151844}}の際には、藩主自ら岡部家の{{jalink|菩提寺|Q1084697}}である{{jalink|泉光寺|Q17222155}}とともに参拝された。{{jalink|廃藩置県|Q331905}}により岸和田藩が消滅に伴い、藩の一ノ宮ではなくなったが、地元民により{{jalink|阿間河谷}}の一ノ宮として今でも祀られている。 === 祭神 === *{{jalink|武内宿禰|Q1073668}}(たけうちのすくね) *{{jalink|波多氏 (古代)|Q11555455|波多八代宿禰}}(はたやしろのすくね) *{{jalink|建御名方命}}(たけみなかたのかみ)　‐　神須屋社({{jalink|諏訪神社|Q2370082}})より合祀 *{{jalink|素盞嗚命}}(すさのおのみこと)　‐　真上社({{jalink|弥栄神社|Q11487447}})より合祀 *{{jalink|菅原道真|Q382005}}(すがわらのみちざね) ‐　神須屋社({{jalink|天神神社|Q11443287}})より合祀 *{{jalink|市杵島比売命}}(いちきしまひめ)　‐ 極楽寺社({{jalink|厳島神社|Q191763}})より合祀 *{{jalink|武甕槌命}}(たけみかづち)　‐　流木社({{jalink|鹿島神社|Q11677154}})より合祀 *祭神不詳だか流木社より四十九神社({{jalink|落合神社}})が合祀されている。 - 戦でなくなった四十九人の首を埋めたという伝説がある。 === 例祭 === 10月の{{jalink|体育の日}}に{{jalink|岸和田十月祭礼|Q11475809}}で東岸和田だんじり祭りの{{jalink|宮入}}が行われる。 地車は{{jalink|八田町 (岸和田市)||八田町}}・{{jalink|真上町}}・{{jalink|神須屋町}}・{{jalink|極楽寺町}}・{{jalink|流木町}}計5台の宮入する。 だんじり祭りにはとても珍しい{{jalink|神輿渡御}}がある。 だんじりの宮出しに行われ、神輿担ぐのは当番町(最後に宮入をする町)であり五町持ち回りで、だんじりはお神輿に続いて{{jalink|御旅所|Q11489117}}までお供する。 御旅所は諏訪神社跡(神須屋町)にある。 先代のお神輿は{{jalink|岸城神社|Q119929652}}が造ったが神輿を担ぐには格式が低かったため、矢代寸神社が購入したもの。 === 境内社 === *{{jalink|稲荷神社|Q514480}} - {{jalink|白高大明神}}と{{jalink|光良大明神}}が祀られている。 *{{jalink|道祖神|Q269515|道祖神社}}(足神様) ‐ 足の悪い人、痛い人が石を撫でてたら治るとされている。 *{{jalink|弁財天}} - 流木社({{jalink|弁財天社}})より{{jalink|遷宮|Q2271023}} === アクセス === {{jalink|南海本線|Q59453}}{{jalink|岸和田駅|Q6416611}}又は{{jalink|阪和線|Q1154800}}{{jalink|東岸和田駅|Q857440}}より{{jalink|南海ウイングバス|Q11408142}}641,642,643,644系統に乗車し、バス停「一の宮」下車 バス停より南へ徒歩3分 === 脚注 === {{Reflist}} === 関連項目 === *{{jalink|岸和田十月祭礼|Q11475809}} *{{jalink|阿理莫神社}} - 大阪府貝塚市久保に鎮座。阿間河荘の豪族である安幕(アマカ)氏由来の神社。鎮座が507年で当社ととても近い。 *{{jalink|行遇堂|Q17225570}}（神須屋町) ‐ 旧{{jalink|阿間河荘}}の神事が行われる時下字（極楽寺・畑・流木）と上字（八田・神須屋・真上）がこのお堂で行き合って矢代寸神社にお参りしていた。 *{{jalink|真上八大龍王神社}}（真上町) ‐ 旧弥栄神社の跡地に土取りをしていた人が埋もれてなくなる事故が起きたことから、戦後{{jalink|八大龍王}}と{{jalink|産土神|Q1392097}}をお祀りされている。 *{{jalink|海岸寺跡}}(貝塚市半田町) ‐ 岸和田藩岡部宣勝が徳川家を供養するために建立した寺院。明治2年に廃寺。一圓法師大徳の墓石に阿間河一宮(矢代寸神社)・第十一世と刻まれている。 {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:やしろきしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=大阪府}} == Categories == {{wikidata link|Q17221560}} == Japanese content == {{Source citation needed| date = December 2024}} {{Shrine |Name= Yashirogi Shrine |Image= [[File:Yashirogi Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location= {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Kishiwada City|Q740456}} 357 Hatta-chō |Coordinates= {{WikiCoordinates2DMS|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Takenouchi no Sukune, Hata Yashiro no Sukune
(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{jalink|Shikinai shrine}}; former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{jalink|Kishiwada City|Q740456}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{jalink|Kamisuyachō}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{jalink|Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{jalink|Habiki Shrine}}. === History === * {{jalink|503|Q30975}} (Year 1 of {{jalink|Emperor Keitai|Q349277}}): The shrine is established. * {{jalink|1090|Q19466}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{jalink|Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|Retired Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{jalink|Kumano pilgrimage}}. * {{jalink|1640|Q6901}} (Kan’ei 17): {{jalink|Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826}} enters the {{jalink|Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469}} of {{jalink|Izumi Province|Q1047117}} from the {{jalink|Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415}} of {{jalink|Settsu Province|Q864163}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{jalink|1737|Q6993}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{jalink|Yoshida family|Q11413722|Kyoto Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. * {{jalink|1907|Q2048}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{jalink|Arimaka Village|Q11516636}}, {{jalink|Sennan District|Q1205635}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{jalink|Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265}}, in the so-called “{{jalink|Shrine consolidation|Q11590700}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{jalink|Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{jalink|Habiki Shrine}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{jalink|Yamabara Sakutarō}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{jalink|Sōsha}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{jalink|Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839}}, there was the {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}} of the highly ranked {{jalink|Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{jalink|Yodo Domain|Q844014}} in {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{jalink|daimyō succession}} and {{jalink|sankin-kōtai|Q1151844}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{jalink|Senko-ji|Q17222155}}, the {{jalink|family temple|Q1084697}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{jalink|Abolition of the han system|Q331905}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}} * {{jalink|Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{jalink|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{jalink|Suwa Shrine|Q2370082}}) * {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{jalink|Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447}}) * {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{jalink|Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287}}) * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{jalink|Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763}}) * {{jalink|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{jalink|Kashima Shrine|Q11677154}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{jalink|Ochiai Shrine}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{jalink|Sports Day}} holiday in October, the {{jalink|Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{jalink|Miyairi}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{jalink|Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)||Hatta-chō}}, {{jalink|Magami-chō}}, {{jalink|Kamisuyachō}}, {{jalink|Gokurakuji-chō}}, and {{jalink|Nagaregi-chō}}. The festival also features the very rare {{jalink|portable shrine procession}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{jalink|Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} – Enshrines {{jalink|Shirotaka Daimyōjin}} and {{jalink|Kōryō Daimyōjin}}. * {{jalink|Dōsojin|Q269515|Dōso Shrine}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{jalink|Benzaiten}} – Transferred ({{jalink|Shikinen sengū|Q2271023}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{jalink|Benzaiten Shrine}}). === Access === From {{jalink|Kishiwada Station|Q6416611}} on the {{jalink|Nankai Main Line|Q59453}} or {{jalink|Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440}} on the {{jalink|Hanwa Line|Q1154800}}, take the {{jalink|Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{jalink|Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809}} * {{jalink|Arima Shrine}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{jalink|Gyōgūdō|Q17225570}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{jalink|Amagawa Estate}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{jalink|Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{jalink|Hachidai Ryūō}} and the {{jalink|Ujigami|Q1392097}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{jalink|Kaigan-ji ruins}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yashirogijinja}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]] [[Category:need translation]]

Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{jalink|Sōsha}} of the Amagawa Estate. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{jalink|Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} – Enshrines {{jalink|Shirotaka Daimyōjin}} and {{jalink|Kōryō Daimyōjin}}. * {{jalink|Dōsojin|Q269515|Dōso Shrine}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{jalink|Benzaiten}} – Transferred ({{jalink|Shikinen sengū|Q2271023}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{jalink|Benzaiten Shrine}}). === Access === From {{jalink|Kishiwada Station|Q6416611}} on the {{jalink|Nankai Main Line|Q59453}} or {{jalink|Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440}} on the {{jalink|Hanwa Line|Q1154800}}, take the {{jalink|Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{jalink|Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809}} * {{jalink|Arima Shrine}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{jalink|Gyōgūdō|Q17225570}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{jalink|Amagawa Estate}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{jalink|Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{jalink|Hachidai Ryūō}} and the {{jalink|Ujigami|Q1392097}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{jalink|Kaigan-ji ruins}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yashirogijinja}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

{{wikidata link|Q17221560}} == Japanese content == {{Source citation needed| date = December 2024}} {{Shrine |Name= Yashirogi Shrine |Image= [[File:Yashirogi Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location= {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Kishiwada City|Q740456}} 357 Hatta-chō |Coordinates= {{WikiCoordinates2DMS|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Takenouchi no Sukune, Hata Yashiro no Sukune
(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{jalink|Shikinai shrine}}; former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{jalink|Kishiwada City|Q740456}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{jalink|Kamisuyachō}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{jalink|Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{jalink|Habiki Shrine}}. === History === * {{jalink|503|Q30975}} (Year 1 of {{jalink|Emperor Keitai|Q349277}}): The shrine is established. * {{jalink|1090|Q19466}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{jalink|Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|Retired Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{jalink|Kumano pilgrimage}}. * {{jalink|1640|Q6901}} (Kan’ei 17): {{jalink|Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826}} enters the {{jalink|Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469}} of {{jalink|Izumi Province|Q1047117}} from the {{jalink|Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415}} of {{jalink|Settsu Province|Q864163}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{jalink|1737|Q6993}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{jalink|Yoshida family|Q11413722|Kyoto Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. * {{jalink|1907|Q2048}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{jalink|Arimaka Village|Q11516636}}, {{jalink|Sennan District|Q1205635}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{jalink|Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265}}, in the so-called “{{jalink|Shrine consolidation|Q11590700}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{jalink|Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{jalink|Habiki Shrine}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{jalink|Yamabara Sakutarō}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{jalink|Sōsha}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{jalink|Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839}}, there was the {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}} of the highly ranked {{jalink|Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{jalink|Yodo Domain|Q844014}} in {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{jalink|daimyō succession}} and {{jalink|sankin-kōtai|Q1151844}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{jalink|Senko-ji|Q17222155}}, the {{jalink|family temple|Q1084697}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{jalink|Abolition of the han system|Q331905}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}} * {{jalink|Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{jalink|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{jalink|Suwa Shrine|Q2370082}}) * {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{jalink|Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447}}) * {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{jalink|Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287}}) * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{jalink|Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763}}) * {{jalink|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{jalink|Kashima Shrine|Q11677154}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{jalink|Ochiai Shrine}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{jalink|Sports Day}} holiday in October, the {{jalink|Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{jalink|Miyairi}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{jalink|Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)||Hatta-chō}}, {{jalink|Magami-chō}}, {{jalink|Kamisuyachō}}, {{jalink|Gokurakuji-chō}}, and {{jalink|Nagaregi-chō}}. The festival also features the very rare {{jalink|portable shrine procession}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{jalink|Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} – Enshrines {{jalink|Shirotaka Daimyōjin}} and {{jalink|Kōryō Daimyōjin}}. * {{jalink|Dōsojin|Q269515|Dōso Shrine}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{jalink|Benzaiten}} – Transferred ({{jalink|Shikinen sengū|Q2271023}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{jalink|Benzaiten Shrine}}). === Access === From {{jalink|Kishiwada Station|Q6416611}} on the {{jalink|Nankai Main Line|Q59453}} or {{jalink|Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440}} on the {{jalink|Hanwa Line|Q1154800}}, take the {{jalink|Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{jalink|Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809}} * {{jalink|Arima Shrine}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{jalink|Gyōgūdō|Q17225570}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{jalink|Amagawa Estate}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{jalink|Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{jalink|Hachidai Ryūō}} and the {{jalink|Ujigami|Q1392097}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{jalink|Kaigan-ji ruins}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yashirogijinja}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{jalink|Kamisuyachō}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{jalink|Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{jalink|Habiki Shrine}}. === History === * {{jalink|503|Q30975}} (Year 1 of {{jalink|Emperor Keitai|Q349277}}): The shrine is established. * {{jalink|1090|Q19466}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{jalink|Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|Retired Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{jalink|Kumano pilgrimage}}. * {{jalink|1640|Q6901}} (Kan’ei 17): {{jalink|Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826}} enters the {{jalink|Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469}} of {{jalink|Izumi Province|Q1047117}} from the {{jalink|Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415}} of {{jalink|Settsu Province|Q864163}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{jalink|1737|Q6993}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{jalink|Yoshida family|Q11413722|Kyoto Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. * {{jalink|1907|Q2048}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{jalink|Arimaka Village|Q11516636}}, {{jalink|Sennan District|Q1205635}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{jalink|Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265}}, in the so-called “{{jalink|Shrine consolidation|Q11590700}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{jalink|Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{jalink|Habiki Shrine}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{jalink|Yamabara Sakutarō}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{jalink|Sōsha}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{jalink|Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839}}, there was the {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}} of the highly ranked {{jalink|Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{jalink|Yodo Domain|Q844014}} in {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{jalink|daimyō succession}} and {{jalink|sankin-kōtai|Q1151844}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{jalink|Senko-ji|Q17222155}}, the {{jalink|family temple|Q1084697}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{jalink|Abolition of the han system|Q331905}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}} * {{jalink|Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{jalink|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{jalink|Suwa Shrine|Q2370082}}) * {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{jalink|Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447}}) * {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{jalink|Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287}}) * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{jalink|Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763}}) * {{jalink|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{jalink|Kashima Shrine|Q11677154}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{jalink|Ochiai Shrine}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{jalink|Sports Day}} holiday in October, the {{jalink|Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{jalink|Miyairi}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{jalink|Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)||Hatta-chō}}, {{jalink|Magami-chō}}, {{jalink|Kamisuyachō}}, {{jalink|Gokurakuji-chō}}, and {{jalink|Nagaregi-chō}}. The festival also features the very rare {{jalink|portable shrine procession}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{jalink|Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} – Enshrines {{jalink|Shirotaka Daimyōjin}} and {{jalink|Kōryō Daimyōjin}}. * {{jalink|Dōsojin|Q269515|Dōso Shrine}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{jalink|Benzaiten}} – Transferred ({{jalink|Shikinen sengū|Q2271023}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{jalink|Benzaiten Shrine}}). === Access === From {{jalink|Kishiwada Station|Q6416611}} on the {{jalink|Nankai Main Line|Q59453}} or {{jalink|Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440}} on the {{jalink|Hanwa Line|Q1154800}}, take the {{jalink|Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{jalink|Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809}} * {{jalink|Arima Shrine}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{jalink|Gyōgūdō|Q17225570}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{jalink|Amagawa Estate}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{jalink|Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{jalink|Hachidai Ryūō}} and the {{jalink|Ujigami|Q1392097}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{jalink|Kaigan-ji ruins}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yashirogijinja}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{jalink|Shikinai shrine}}; former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{jalink|Kishiwada City|Q740456}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{jalink|Kamisuyachō}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{jalink|Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{jalink|Habiki Shrine}}. === History === * {{jalink|503|Q30975}} (Year 1 of {{jalink|Emperor Keitai|Q349277}}): The shrine is established. * {{jalink|1090|Q19466}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{jalink|Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|Retired Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{jalink|Kumano pilgrimage}}. * {{jalink|1640|Q6901}} (Kan’ei 17): {{jalink|Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826}} enters the {{jalink|Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469}} of {{jalink|Izumi Province|Q1047117}} from the {{jalink|Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415}} of {{jalink|Settsu Province|Q864163}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{jalink|1737|Q6993}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{jalink|Yoshida family|Q11413722|Kyoto Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. * {{jalink|1907|Q2048}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{jalink|Arimaka Village|Q11516636}}, {{jalink|Sennan District|Q1205635}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{jalink|Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265}}, in the so-called “{{jalink|Shrine consolidation|Q11590700}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{jalink|Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{jalink|Habiki Shrine}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{jalink|Yamabara Sakutarō}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{jalink|Sōsha}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{jalink|Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839}}, there was the {{jalink|Otabisho|Q11489117}} of the highly ranked {{jalink|Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{jalink|Yodo Domain|Q844014}} in {{jalink|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{jalink|daimyō succession}} and {{jalink|sankin-kōtai|Q1151844}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{jalink|Senko-ji|Q17222155}}, the {{jalink|family temple|Q1084697}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{jalink|Abolition of the han system|Q331905}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{jalink|Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668}} * {{jalink|Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{jalink|Takeminakata-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{jalink|Suwa Shrine|Q2370082}}) * {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{jalink|Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447}}) * {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{jalink|Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287}}) * {{jalink|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{jalink|Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763}}) * {{jalink|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{jalink|Kashima Shrine|Q11677154}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{jalink|Ochiai Shrine}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. Torii gate and worship hall |Location= {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456}} 357 Hatta-chō |Coordinates= {{WikiCoordinates2DMS|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Takenouchi no Sukune, Hata Yashiro no Sukune
(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{ill||lt=Shikinai shrine|UNKNOWN}}; former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{ill||lt=Kamisuyachō|UNKNOWN}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{ill||lt=Habiki Shrine|UNKNOWN}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=503|Q30975|WD=Q30975}} (Year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277}}): The shrine is established. * {{ill|lt=1090|Q19466|WD=Q19466}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|WD=Q60949|lt=Retired Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{ill||lt=Kumano pilgrimage|UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=1640|Q6901|WD=Q6901}} (Kan’ei 17): {{ill|lt=Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826|WD=Q11473826}} enters the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469}} of {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117}} from the {{ill|lt=Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415|WD=Q10863415}} of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{ill|lt=Yoshida family|Q11413722|WD=Q11413722|lt=Kyoto Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|lt=Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. * {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{ill|lt=Arimaka Village|Q11516636|WD=Q11516636}}, {{ill|lt=Sennan District|Q1205635|WD=Q1205635}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265|WD=Q1142265}}, in the so-called “{{ill|lt=Shrine consolidation|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{ill|lt=Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777|WD=Q11423777}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{ill||lt=Habiki Shrine|UNKNOWN}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{ill||lt=Yamabara Sakutarō|UNKNOWN}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{ill||lt=Sōsha|UNKNOWN}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill||lt=daimyō succession|UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill||lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082}}) * {{ill||lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287}}) * {{ill||lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763}}) * {{ill||lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill||lt=Ochiai Shrine|UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{ill||lt=Sports Day|UNKNOWN}} holiday in October, the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{ill||lt=Miyairi|UNKNOWN}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{ill|lt=Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)||WD=|lt=Hatta-chō}}, {{ill||lt=Magami-chō|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Kamisuyachō|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Gokurakuji-chō|UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill||lt=Nagaregi-chō|UNKNOWN}}. The festival also features the very rare {{ill||lt=portable shrine procession|UNKNOWN}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{ill|lt=Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652|WD=Q119929652}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} – Enshrines {{ill||lt=Shirotaka Daimyōjin|UNKNOWN}} and {{ill||lt=Kōryō Daimyōjin|UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōso Shrine}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{ill||lt=Benzaiten|UNKNOWN}} – Transferred ({{ill|lt=Shikinen sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill||lt=Benzaiten Shrine|UNKNOWN}}). === Access === From {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Station|Q6416611|WD=Q6416611}} on the {{ill|lt=Nankai Main Line|Q59453|WD=Q59453}} or {{ill|lt=Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440|WD=Q857440}} on the {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142|WD=Q11408142}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809}} * {{ill||lt=Arima Shrine|UNKNOWN}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{ill|lt=Gyōgūdō|Q17225570|WD=Q17225570}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{ill||lt=Amagawa Estate|UNKNOWN}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{ill||lt=Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine|UNKNOWN}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{ill||lt=Hachidai Ryūō|UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=Ujigami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{ill||lt=Kaigan-ji ruins|UNKNOWN}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yashirogijinja}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265|WD=Q1142265}}, in the so-called “{{ill|lt=Shrine consolidation|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{ill|lt=Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777|WD=Q11423777}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|UNKNOWN}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamabara Sakutarō|UNKNOWN}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōsha|UNKNOWN}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daimyō succession|UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ochiai Shrine|UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|UNKNOWN}} holiday in October, the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miyairi|UNKNOWN}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{ill|lt=Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)||WD=|lt=Hatta-chō}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami-chō|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gokurakuji-chō|UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagaregi-chō|UNKNOWN}}. The festival also features the very rare {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=portable shrine procession|UNKNOWN}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{ill|lt=Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652|WD=Q119929652}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} – Enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shirotaka Daimyōjin|UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōryō Daimyōjin|UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōso Shrine}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Benzaiten|UNKNOWN}} – Transferred ({{ill|lt=Shikinen sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Benzaiten Shrine|UNKNOWN}}). === Access === From {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Station|Q6416611|WD=Q6416611}} on the {{ill|lt=Nankai Main Line|Q59453|WD=Q59453}} or {{ill|lt=Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440|WD=Q857440}} on the {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142|WD=Q11408142}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arima Shrine|UNKNOWN}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{ill|lt=Gyōgūdō|Q17225570|WD=Q17225570}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amagawa Estate|UNKNOWN}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine|UNKNOWN}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachidai Ryūō|UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=Ujigami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kaigan-ji ruins|UNKNOWN}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yashirogijinja}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

{{wikidata link|Q17221560}} == Japanese content == {{Source citation needed| date = December 2024}} {{Shrine |Name= Yashirogi Shrine |Image= [[File:Yashirogi Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location= {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456}} 357 Hatta-chō |Coordinates= {{WikiCoordinates2DMS|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Takenouchi no Sukune, Hata Yashiro no Sukune
(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}; former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=503|Q30975|WD=Q30975}} (Year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277}}): The shrine is established. * {{ill|lt=1090|Q19466|WD=Q19466}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|WD=Q60949|lt=Retired Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano pilgrimage|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=1640|Q6901|WD=Q6901}} (Kan’ei 17): {{ill|lt=Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826|WD=Q11473826}} enters the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469}} of {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117}} from the {{ill|lt=Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415|WD=Q10863415}} of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{ill|lt=Yoshida family|Q11413722|WD=Q11413722|lt=Kyoto Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|lt=Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. * {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{ill|lt=Arimaka Village|Q11516636|WD=Q11516636}}, {{ill|lt=Sennan District|Q1205635|WD=Q1205635}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265|WD=Q1142265}}, in the so-called “{{ill|lt=Shrine consolidation|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{ill|lt=Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777|WD=Q11423777}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamabara Sakutarō|1=UNKNOWN}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōsha|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daimyō succession|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ochiai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}} holiday in October, the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miyairi|1=UNKNOWN}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{ill|lt=Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652|WD=Q119929652}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} – Enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shirotaka Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōryō Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōso Shrine}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Benzaiten|1=UNKNOWN}} – Transferred ({{ill|lt=Shikinen sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Benzaiten Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}). === Access === From {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Station|Q6416611|WD=Q6416611}} on the {{ill|lt=Nankai Main Line|Q59453|WD=Q59453}} or {{ill|lt=Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440|WD=Q857440}} on the {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142|WD=Q11408142}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arima Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture.
