In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=503|Q30975|WD=Q30975}} (Year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277}}): The shrine is established. * {{ill|lt=1090|Q19466|WD=Q19466}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|WD=Q60949|lt=Retired Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano pilgrimage|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=1640|Q6901|WD=Q6901}} (Kan’ei 17): {{ill|lt=Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826|WD=Q11473826}} enters the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469}} of {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117}} from the {{ill|lt=Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415|WD=Q10863415}} of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{ill|lt=Yoshida family|Q11413722|WD=Q11413722|lt=Kyoto Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|lt=Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. * {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{ill|lt=Arimaka Village|Q11516636|WD=Q11516636}}, {{ill|lt=Sennan District|Q1205635|WD=Q1205635}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265|WD=Q1142265}}, in the so-called “{{ill|lt=Shrine consolidation|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{ill|lt=Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777|WD=Q11423777}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamabara Sakutarō|1=UNKNOWN}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōsha|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daimyō succession|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=q|WD=Q9150391}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082}}) * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ochiai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}} holiday in October, the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miyairi|1=UNKNOWN}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{ill|lt=Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Hatta-chō}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gokurakuji-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagaregi-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}. The festival also features the very rare {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=portable shrine procession|1=UNKNOWN}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{ill|lt=Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652|WD=Q119929652}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} – Enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shirotaka Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōryō Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōso Shrine}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=UNKNOWN}} – Transferred ({{ill|lt=Shikinen sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}). === Access === From {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Station|Q6416611|WD=Q6416611}} on the {{ill|lt=Nankai Main Line|Q59453|WD=Q59453}} or {{ill|lt=Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440|WD=Q857440}} on the {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142|WD=Q11408142}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arima Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{ill|lt=Gyōgūdō|Q17225570|WD=Q17225570}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amagawa Estate|1=UNKNOWN}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachidai Ryūō|1=UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=Ujigami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kaigan-ji ruins|1=UNKNOWN}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yashirogijinja}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

Torii gate and worship hall |Location= {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}} 357 Hatta-chō |Coordinates= {{WikiCoordinates2DMS|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Takenouchi no Sukune, Hata Yashiro no Sukune
(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}; former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=503|Q30975|WD=Q30975|1=503}} (Year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}): The shrine is established. * {{ill|lt=1090|Q19466|WD=Q19466|1=1090}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|WD=Q60949|lt=Retired Emperor Shirakawa|1=Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano pilgrimage|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=1640|Q6901|WD=Q6901|1=1640}} (Kan’ei 17): {{ill|lt=Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826|WD=Q11473826|1=Okabe Nobukatsu}} enters the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117|1=Izumi Province}} from the {{ill|lt=Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415|WD=Q10863415|1=Takatsuki Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163|1=Settsu Province}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993|1=1737}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{ill|lt=Yoshida family|Q11413722|WD=Q11413722|lt=Kyoto Yoshida family|1=Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|lt=Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. * {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{ill|lt=Arimaka Village|Q11516636|WD=Q11516636|1=Amaka}}, {{ill|lt=Sennan District|Q1205635|WD=Q1205635|1=Sennan District, Osaka}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265|WD=Q1142265|1=Home Ministry}}, in the so-called “{{ill|lt=Shrine consolidation|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{ill|lt=Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777|WD=Q11423777|1=Habugō}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamabara Sakutarō|1=UNKNOWN}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōsha|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839|1=Kishiwadahama}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137|1=Tsugawa Shrine}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014|1=Yodo Domain}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daimyō succession|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155|1=Senkō-ji Temple}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697|1=Bodaiji}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905|1=Abolition of the han system}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune|1=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082|1=Suwa Shrine}}) * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447|1=Yasaka Shrine}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287|1=Tenjin Jinja}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763|1=Itsukushima Shrine}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154|1=Kashima Shrines}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ochiai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}} holiday in October, the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miyairi|1=UNKNOWN}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{ill|lt=Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Hatta-chō}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gokurakuji-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagaregi-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}. The festival also features the very rare {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=portable shrine procession|1=UNKNOWN}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{ill|lt=Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652|WD=Q119929652|1=Kishiki Shrine}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} – Enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shirotaka Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōryō Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōso Shrine|1=Dōsojin}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}} – Transferred ({{ill|lt=Shikinen sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten Shrine|1=Benzaiten}}). === Access === From {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Station|Q6416611|WD=Q6416611|1=Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Nankai Main Line|Q59453|WD=Q59453|1=Nankai Main Line}} or {{ill|lt=Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440|WD=Q857440|1=Higashi-Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|1=Hanwa Line}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142|WD=Q11408142|1=Nankai Wing Bus}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arima Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{ill|lt=Gyōgūdō|Q17225570|WD=Q17225570|1=Yukiai-dō Temple}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amagawa Estate|1=UNKNOWN}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachidai Ryūō|1=UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=Ujigami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kaigan-ji ruins|1=UNKNOWN}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yashirogijinja}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

Torii gate and worship hall |Location= {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}} 357 Hatta-chō |Coordinates= {{WikiCoordinates2DMS|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Takenouchi no Sukune, Hata Yashiro no Sukune
(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}; former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=503|Q30975|WD=Q30975|1=503}} (Year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}): The shrine is established. * {{ill|lt=1090|Q19466|WD=Q19466|1=1090}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|WD=Q60949|lt=Retired Emperor Shirakawa|1=Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano pilgrimage|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=1640|Q6901|WD=Q6901|1=1640}} (Kan’ei 17): {{ill|lt=Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826|WD=Q11473826|1=Okabe Nobukatsu}} enters the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117|1=Izumi Province}} from the {{ill|lt=Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415|WD=Q10863415|1=Takatsuki Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163|1=Settsu Province}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993|1=1737}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{ill|lt=Yoshida family|Q11413722|WD=Q11413722|lt=Kyoto Yoshida family|1=Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|lt=Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. * {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{ill|lt=Arimaka Village|Q11516636|WD=Q11516636|1=Amaka}}, {{ill|lt=Sennan District|Q1205635|WD=Q1205635|1=Sennan District, Osaka}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265|WD=Q1142265|1=Home Ministry}}, in the so-called “{{ill|lt=Shrine consolidation|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{ill|lt=Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777|WD=Q11423777|1=Habugō}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamabara Sakutarō|1=UNKNOWN}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōsha|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839|1=Kishiwadahama}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137|1=Tsugawa Shrine}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014|1=Yodo Domain}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daimyō succession|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155|1=Senkō-ji Temple}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697|1=Bodaiji}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905|1=Abolition of the han system}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune|1=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082|1=Suwa Shrine}}) * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447|1=Yasaka Shrine}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287|1=Tenjin Jinja}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763|1=Itsukushima Shrine}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154|1=Kashima Shrines}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ochiai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{ill|lt=Gyōgūdō|Q17225570|WD=Q17225570|1=Yukiai-dō Temple}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amagawa Estate|1=UNKNOWN}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachidai Ryūō|1=UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=Ujigami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kaigan-ji ruins|1=UNKNOWN}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yashirogijinja}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

It is located in [[Izumi Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:矢代寸神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17221560}} == Japanese content == {{Source citation needed| date = December 2024}} {{Shrine |Name= Yashirogi Shrine |Image= [[File:Yashirogi Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location= {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}} 357 Hatta-chō |Coordinates= {{WikiCoordinates2DMS|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Takenouchi no Sukune, Hata Yashiro no Sukune
(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}; former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=503|Q30975|WD=Q30975|1=503}} (Year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}): The shrine is established. * {{ill|lt=1090|Q19466|WD=Q19466|1=1090}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|WD=Q60949|lt=Retired Emperor Shirakawa|1=Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano pilgrimage|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=1640|Q6901|WD=Q6901|1=1640}} (Kan’ei 17): {{ill|lt=Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826|WD=Q11473826|1=Okabe Nobukatsu}} enters the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117|1=Izumi Province}} from the {{ill|lt=Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415|WD=Q10863415|1=Takatsuki Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163|1=Settsu Province}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993|1=1737}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{ill|lt=Yoshida family|Q11413722|WD=Q11413722|lt=Kyoto Yoshida family|1=Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|lt=Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. * {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{ill|lt=Arimaka Village|Q11516636|WD=Q11516636|1=Amaka}}, {{ill|lt=Sennan District|Q1205635|WD=Q1205635|1=Sennan District, Osaka}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265|WD=Q1142265|1=Home Ministry}}, in the so-called “{{ill|lt=Shrine consolidation|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{ill|lt=Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777|WD=Q11423777|1=Habugō}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamabara Sakutarō|1=UNKNOWN}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōsha|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839|1=Kishiwadahama}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137|1=Tsugawa Shrine}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014|1=Yodo Domain}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daimyō succession|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155|1=Senkō-ji Temple}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697|1=Bodaiji}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905|1=Abolition of the han system}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune|1=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082|1=Suwa Shrine}}) * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447|1=Yasaka Shrine}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287|1=Tenjin Jinja}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763|1=Itsukushima Shrine}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154|1=Kashima Shrines}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ochiai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}} holiday in October, the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miyairi|1=UNKNOWN}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{ill|lt=Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Hatta-chō}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gokurakuji-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagaregi-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}. The festival also features the very rare {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=portable shrine procession|1=UNKNOWN}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{ill|lt=Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652|WD=Q119929652|1=Kishiki Shrine}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} – Enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shirotaka Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōryō Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōso Shrine|1=Dōsojin}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}} – Transferred ({{ill|lt=Shikinen sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten Shrine|1=Benzaiten}}). === Access === From {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Station|Q6416611|WD=Q6416611|1=Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Nankai Main Line|Q59453|WD=Q59453|1=Nankai Main Line}} or {{ill|lt=Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440|WD=Q857440|1=Higashi-Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|1=Hanwa Line}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142|WD=Q11408142|1=Nankai Wing Bus}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arima Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{ill|lt=Gyōgūdō|Q17225570|WD=Q17225570|1=Yukiai-dō Temple}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amagawa Estate|1=UNKNOWN}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachidai Ryūō|1=UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=Ujigami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kaigan-ji ruins|1=UNKNOWN}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:和泉国の式内社]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

Shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii gate and worship hall |Location= {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}} 357 Hatta-chō |Coordinates= {{WikiCoordinates2DMS|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Takenouchi no Sukune, Hata Yashiro no Sukune
(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}; former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=503|Q30975|WD=Q30975|1=503}} (Year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}): The shrine is established. * {{ill|lt=1090|Q19466|WD=Q19466|1=1090}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|WD=Q60949|lt=Retired Emperor Shirakawa|1=Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano pilgrimage|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=1640|Q6901|WD=Q6901|1=1640}} (Kan’ei 17): {{ill|lt=Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826|WD=Q11473826|1=Okabe Nobukatsu}} enters the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117|1=Izumi Province}} from the {{ill|lt=Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415|WD=Q10863415|1=Takatsuki Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163|1=Settsu Province}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993|1=1737}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{ill|lt=Yoshida family|Q11413722|WD=Q11413722|lt=Kyoto Yoshida family|1=Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|lt=Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. * {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{ill|lt=Arimaka Village|Q11516636|WD=Q11516636|1=Amaka}}, {{ill|lt=Sennan District|Q1205635|WD=Q1205635|1=Sennan District, Osaka}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265|WD=Q1142265|1=Home Ministry}}, in the so-called “{{ill|lt=Shrine consolidation|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{ill|lt=Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777|WD=Q11423777|1=Habugō}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamabara Sakutarō|1=UNKNOWN}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōsha|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839|1=Kishiwadahama}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137|1=Tsugawa Shrine}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014|1=Yodo Domain}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daimyō succession|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155|1=Senkō-ji Temple}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697|1=Bodaiji}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905|1=Abolition of the han system}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune|1=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082|1=Suwa Shrine}}) * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447|1=Yasaka Shrine}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287|1=Tenjin Jinja}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763|1=Itsukushima Shrine}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154|1=Kashima Shrines}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ochiai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}} holiday in October, the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miyairi|1=UNKNOWN}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{ill|lt=Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Hatta-chō}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gokurakuji-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagaregi-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}. The festival also features the very rare {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=portable shrine procession|1=UNKNOWN}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{ill|lt=Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652|WD=Q119929652|1=Kishiki Shrine}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} – Enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shirotaka Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōryō Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōso Shrine|1=Dōsojin}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}} – Transferred ({{ill|lt=Shikinen sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten Shrine|1=Benzaiten}}). === Access === From {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Station|Q6416611|WD=Q6416611|1=Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Nankai Main Line|Q59453|WD=Q59453|1=Nankai Main Line}} or {{ill|lt=Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440|WD=Q857440|1=Higashi-Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|1=Hanwa Line}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142|WD=Q11408142|1=Nankai Wing Bus}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arima Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{ill|lt=Gyōgūdō|Q17225570|WD=Q17225570|1=Yukiai-dō Temple}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amagawa Estate|1=UNKNOWN}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachidai Ryūō|1=UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=Ujigami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kaigan-ji ruins|1=UNKNOWN}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}} of the same city. During the Edo period, it served as the Ichinomiya of the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}}, and when the domain lord changed, it was customary to pay respects at all shrines within the domain; later, this was limited to worship at this shrine alone. Although it is a shrine with a long history, it currently has no resident priest, and is administered concurrently by the chief priest (the Sakai family) of the nearby {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=503|Q30975|WD=Q30975|1=503}} (Year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keitai|Q349277|WD=Q349277|1=Emperor Keitai}}): The shrine is established. * {{ill|lt=1090|Q19466|WD=Q19466|1=1090}} (Kanji 4): In January, {{ill|lt=Emperor Shirakawa|Q60949|WD=Q60949|lt=Retired Emperor Shirakawa|1=Emperor Shirakawa}} visits the shrine during his {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kumano pilgrimage|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=1640|Q6901|WD=Q6901|1=1640}} (Kan’ei 17): {{ill|lt=Okabe Nobukatsu|Q11473826|WD=Q11473826|1=Okabe Nobukatsu}} enters the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Domain|Q3034469|WD=Q3034469|1=Kishiwada Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Izumi Province|Q1047117|WD=Q1047117|1=Izumi Province}} from the {{ill|lt=Takatsuki Domain|Q10863415|WD=Q10863415|1=Takatsuki Domain}} of {{ill|lt=Settsu Province|Q864163|WD=Q864163|1=Settsu Province}} and designates the shrine as the Ichinomiya of the domain. * {{ill|lt=1737|Q6993|WD=Q6993|1=1737}} (Genbun 2): Upon petition to the {{ill|lt=Yoshida family|Q11413722|WD=Q11413722|lt=Kyoto Yoshida family|1=Yoshida family}}, the shrine is granted the title “{{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247|lt=Ichinomiya Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}}.” * {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6): Designated as a {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. * {{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048|1=1907}} (Meiji 40): The Suwa Shrine of Kamisuya in {{ill|lt=Arimaka Village|Q11516636|WD=Q11516636|1=Amaka}}, {{ill|lt=Sennan District|Q1205635|WD=Q1205635|1=Sennan District, Osaka}}, is merged into the shrine. Under the directive of the heads of the Shrine and Religion Bureaus of the {{ill|lt=Ministry of Home Affairs (Japan)|Q1142265|WD=Q1142265|1=Home Ministry}}, in the so-called “{{ill|lt=Shrine consolidation|Q11590700|WD=Q11590700|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}},” the deities of Kamisuya and Magami, as well as those of Gokurakuji, Nagaregi, and Hata in {{ill|lt=Habiki-gō Village|Q11423777|WD=Q11423777|1=Habugō}}, were merged. A policy was also proposed to merge them into {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Habiki Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, located in Habiki of Habiki-gō Village, but opposition led by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yamabara Sakutarō|1=UNKNOWN}} forced the abandonment of this plan. Although the Hata Hata Shrine was merged, it was later restored as an independent shrine. * {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056|1=1908}} (Meiji 41): Designated as a shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements. === Origin of the title Ichinomiya === Since ancient times, the shrine was known as the Ichinomiya as the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sōsha|1=UNKNOWN}} of the Amagawa Estate. In the Edo period, when Okabe Nobukatsu entered the Kishiwada Domain from the Takatsuki Domain, there was no Ichinomiya within his territory. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839|1=Kishiwadahama}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137|1=Tsugawa Shrine}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014|1=Yodo Domain}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daimyō succession|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155|1=Senkō-ji Temple}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697|1=Bodaiji}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905|1=Abolition of the han system}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune|1=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082|1=Suwa Shrine}}) * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447|1=Yasaka Shrine}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287|1=Tenjin Jinja}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763|1=Itsukushima Shrine}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154|1=Kashima Shrines}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ochiai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}} holiday in October, the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miyairi|1=UNKNOWN}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{ill|lt=Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Hatta-chō}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gokurakuji-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagaregi-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}. The festival also features the very rare {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=portable shrine procession|1=UNKNOWN}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{ill|lt=Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652|WD=Q119929652|1=Kishiki Shrine}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} – Enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shirotaka Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōryō Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōso Shrine|1=Dōsojin}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}} – Transferred ({{ill|lt=Shikinen sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten Shrine|1=Benzaiten}}). === Access === From {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Station|Q6416611|WD=Q6416611|1=Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Nankai Main Line|Q59453|WD=Q59453|1=Nankai Main Line}} or {{ill|lt=Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440|WD=Q857440|1=Higashi-Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|1=Hanwa Line}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142|WD=Q11408142|1=Nankai Wing Bus}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arima Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{ill|lt=Gyōgūdō|Q17225570|WD=Q17225570|1=Yukiai-dō Temple}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amagawa Estate|1=UNKNOWN}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachidai Ryūō|1=UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=Ujigami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kaigan-ji ruins|1=UNKNOWN}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:岸和田市の建築物]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]

Torii gate and worship hall |Location= {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}} 357 Hatta-chō |Coordinates= {{WikiCoordinates2DMS|34|26|2.7|N|135|24|6.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities= Takenouchi no Sukune, Hata Yashiro no Sukune
(additionally enshrined) Takeminakata-no-Mikoto, Susanoo-no-Mikoto, Sugawara no Michizane, Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank= {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}; former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded= Year 1 of Emperor Keitai (503 CE) |Main hall style= {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Buildings= Main hall, offering hall, worship hall, sacred offering hall, portable shrine storehouse, treasure storehouse |Alternate name= Ichinomiya |Annual festival= October 10 |Rituals= Danjiri floats, portable shrine |Map= {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Yashirogi Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Yashirogi Shrine''' (やしろぎじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located at 357 Hatta-chō, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada City|Q740456|WD=Q740456|1=Kishiwada, Osaka}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} it is listed as the Yashiro Village Shrine of Junior Fifth Rank, and the area was formerly called Yashiro Village (Yashirogi). The record states that Yashirogi Shrine was enshrined here, with one seat being the main shrine, known as Ichinomiya, located in the present-day Ōaza Hatta, aza Miyauchi, and another seat known as Suwa Shrine or Yashirogi Lower Shrine, which was located in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}} of the same city. In the southern district of the domain, {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Hamamachi|Q11475839|WD=Q11475839|1=Kishiwadahama}}, there was the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}} of the highly ranked {{ill|lt=Tsugawa Shrine|Q11597137|WD=Q11597137|1=Tsugawa Shrine}}, which was the Fourth Shrine of Izumi Province, but Tsugawa Village, where Tsugawa Shrine was located, was shogunate land. Therefore, the Otabisho was relocated to Kita-gaku, Komatsuzato Village (territory of the {{ill|lt=Yodo Domain|Q844014|WD=Q844014|1=Yodo Domain}} in {{ill|lt=Yamashiro Province|Q749276|WD=Q749276|1=Yamashiro Province}}), which was known for the plaque bestowed by Retired Emperor Shirakawa. At that time, a search was conducted for an old and prestigious shrine within the domain, and Yashirogi Shrine was selected and designated as the Ichinomiya of the Kishiwada Domain. During occasions such as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=daimyō succession|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=sankin-kōtai|Q1151844|WD=Q1151844|1=Sankin-kōtai}}, the domain lord himself would worship here together with {{ill|lt=Senko-ji|Q17222155|WD=Q17222155|1=Senkō-ji Temple}}, the {{ill|lt=family temple|Q1084697|WD=Q1084697|1=Bodaiji}} of the Okabe clan. After the abolition of the domains under the {{ill|lt=Abolition of the han system|Q331905|WD=Q331905|1=Abolition of the han system}}, the Kishiwada Domain ceased to exist, and the shrine lost its status as the domain’s Ichinomiya, but it is still worshipped by local residents as the Ichinomiya of the Amagawa Valley. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|Q1073668|WD=Q1073668|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}} * {{ill|lt=Hata clan (ancient)|Q11555455|WD=Q11555455|lt=Hata Yashiro no Sukune|1=Hata Yashiro no Sukune}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata-no-Mikoto|1=Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Suwa Shrine|Q2370082|WD=Q2370082|1=Suwa Shrine}}) * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} – merged from Magami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Yasaka Shrine|Q11487447|WD=Q11487447|1=Yasaka Shrine}}) * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} – merged from Kamisuya Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tenjin Shrine|Q11443287|WD=Q11443287|1=Tenjin Jinja}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|1=Ichikishima-hime}} – merged from Gokurakuji Shrine ({{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shrine|Q191763|WD=Q191763|1=Itsukushima Shrine}}) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} – merged from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q11677154|WD=Q11677154|1=Kashima Shrines}}) * Additionally, the Forty-nine Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ochiai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}) from Nagaregi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is unknown, has been merged. There is a legend that the heads of forty-nine people who died in battle were buried here. === Annual festival === During the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sports Day|1=UNKNOWN}} holiday in October, the {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} is held, featuring the shrine entry ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Miyairi|1=UNKNOWN}}) of the Higashi-Kishiwada Danjiri Festival. A total of five danjiri floats participate from {{ill|lt=Hatta-chō (Kishiwada City)|UNKNOWN|lt=Hatta-chō}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kamisuyachō|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Gokurakuji-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nagaregi-chō|1=UNKNOWN}}. The festival also features the very rare {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=portable shrine procession|1=UNKNOWN}}. The portable shrine procession takes place during the danjiri’s departure from the shrine. The mikoshi is carried by the duty town (the town that enters the shrine last), rotating among the five towns, and the danjiri follow the mikoshi in procession to the {{ill|lt=Otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117|1=Otabisho}}. The Otabisho is located at the former site of Suwa Shrine (Kamisuyachō). The previous mikoshi was made by {{ill|lt=Kishiki Shrine|Q119929652|WD=Q119929652|1=Kishiki Shrine}}, but it was considered of too low a rank to be carried, so Yashirogi Shrine purchased it. === Subordinate shrines === * {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480|1=Inari shrine}} – Enshrines {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shirotaka Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kōryō Daimyōjin|1=UNKNOWN}}. * {{ill|lt=Dōsojin|Q269515|WD=Q269515|lt=Dōso Shrine|1=Dōsojin}} (Ashigami-sama) – It is said that people with foot ailments or pain will be healed by rubbing the stone. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten|1=Benzaiten}} – Transferred ({{ill|lt=Shikinen sengū|Q2271023|WD=Q2271023|1=Sengū}}) from Nagaregi Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q818468|lt=Benzaiten Shrine|1=Benzaiten}}). === Access === From {{ill|lt=Kishiwada Station|Q6416611|WD=Q6416611|1=Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Nankai Main Line|Q59453|WD=Q59453|1=Nankai Main Line}} or {{ill|lt=Higashi-Kishiwada Station|Q857440|WD=Q857440|1=Higashi-Kishiwada Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Hanwa Line|Q1154800|WD=Q1154800|1=Hanwa Line}}, take the {{ill|lt=Nankai Wing Bus|Q11408142|WD=Q11408142|1=Nankai Wing Bus}} routes 641, 642, 643, or 644, get off at the “Ichinomiya” bus stop, then walk south for 3 minutes. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === Related articles === * {{ill|lt=Kishiwada October Festival|Q11475809|WD=Q11475809|1=Kishiwada Danjiri Festival}} * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Arima Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} – Located in Kubo, Kaizuka City, Osaka Prefecture. A shrine associated with the Amaka clan, powerful families of the Amagawa Estate. It was founded in 507, very close in date to this shrine. * {{ill|lt=Gyōgūdō|Q17225570|WD=Q17225570|1=Yukiai-dō Temple}} (Kamisuyachō) – When Shinto rites of the former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amagawa Estate|1=UNKNOWN}} were held, the lower districts (Gokurakuji, Hata, Nagaregi) and upper districts (Hatta, Kamisuya, Magami) would meet at this hall and then proceed together to worship at Yashirogi Shrine. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Magami Hachidai Ryūō Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (Magami-chō) – After an accident in which people collecting earth at the former site of Yasaka Shrine were buried and died, the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hachidai Ryūō|1=UNKNOWN}} and the {{ill|lt=Ujigami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} were enshrined here after the war. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kaigan-ji ruins|1=UNKNOWN}} (Handa-chō, Kaizuka City) – A temple built by Okabe Nobukatsu of the Kishiwada Domain to memorialize the Tokugawa family. Abolished in Meiji 2. The gravestone of the monk Ien Hōshi Daiku bears the inscription “Amagawa Ichinomiya (Yashirogi Shrine), 11th generation.” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=Osaka Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yashirogi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kishiwada, Osaka]] [[Category:岸和田市の歴史]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izumi Province]] [[Category:岸和田藩]]
